blocks:
Ayahs

إِذَا جَآءَكَ ٱلْمُنَـٰفِقُونَ قَالُوا۟ نَشْهَدُ إِنَّكَ لَرَسُولُ ٱللَّهِ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ يَعْلَمُ إِنَّكَ لَرَسُولُهُۥ وَٱللَّهُ يَشْهَدُ إِنَّ ٱلْمُنَـٰفِقِينَ لَكَـٰذِبُونَ (١)
When the hypocrites come to you they say, ‘We bear witness that you are indeed the apostle of Allah.’ Allah knows that you are indeed His Apostle, and Allah bears witness that the hypocrites are indeed liars. (1)
ٱتَّخَذُوٓا۟ أَيْمَـٰنَهُمْ جُنَّةً فَصَدُّوا۟ عَن سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ ۚ إِنَّهُمْ سَآءَ مَا كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ (٢)
They make a shield of their oaths, and bar from the way of Allah. Evil indeed is what they used to do. (2)
ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمْ ءَامَنُوا۟ ثُمَّ كَفَرُوا۟ فَطُبِعَ عَلَىٰ قُلُوبِهِمْ فَهُمْ لَا يَفْقَهُونَ (٣)
That is because they believed and then disbelieved, so their hearts were sealed. Hence, they do not understand. (3)
۞ وَإِذَا رَأَيْتَهُمْ تُعْجِبُكَ أَجْسَامُهُمْ ۖ وَإِن يَقُولُوا۟ تَسْمَعْ لِقَوْلِهِمْ ۖ كَأَنَّهُمْ خُشُبٌ مُّسَنَّدَةٌ ۖ يَحْسَبُونَ كُلَّ صَيْحَةٍ عَلَيْهِمْ ۚ هُمُ ٱلْعَدُوُّ فَٱحْذَرْهُمْ ۚ قَـٰتَلَهُمُ ٱللَّهُ ۖ أَنَّىٰ يُؤْفَكُونَ (٤)
When you see them, their bodies impress you, and if they speak, you listen to their speech. Yet they are like dry logs set reclining [against a wall]. They suppose every cry is directed against them. They are the enemy, so beware of them. May Allah assail them, where do they stray?! (4)

Content of Surah Al-Munafiqun

Surah Al-Munafiqun is among the profound Surahs, whose primary axis of discussion consists of sensitive issues related to the "hypocrites." However, at the end of the Surah, verses are also presented as admonition to the Muslims in various fields.

Altogether, its content can be summarized into four sections:

  1. The signs of the hypocrites, which itself includes several sensitive parts.

  2. Warning the believers against the conspiracies of the hypocrites and the necessity of constant vigilance in this regard.

  3. Warning the believers that the material blessings of the world should not make them heedless of the remembrance of Allah.

  4. Recommendation to spend in the way of Allah and to utilize wealth before death arrives and the fire of regret consumes the human soul.

The reason for naming this Surah "Al-Munafiqun" (The Hypocrites) is also self-evident.

It is noteworthy that, according to what we mentioned in the commentary of Surah Al-Jumu'ah, one of the etiquettes of the Friday prayer is that Surah Al-Jumu'ah be recited in the first rak'ah and Surah Al-Munafiqun in the second rak'ah. This is so that every week, during this great socio-political worship ceremony, Muslims may recall the conspiracies of the hypocrites and remain constantly vigilant against their provocations, sabotages, and sinister plots.

The Virtue of Reciting Surah Al-Munafiqun

In a Hadith from the Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, it is stated: "Whoever recites Surah Al-Munafiqun is cleared of any form of hypocrisy." [^1] And in a Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read: "It is necessary for every believer among our followers (Shi'a) to recite Surah Al-Jumu'ah and 'Sabbih Isma Rabbika al-A'la' on Friday eve, and in the Friday noon prayer, to recite Surah Al-Jumu'ah and Al-Munafiqun." He then added: "When he does so, it is as if he has performed the action of the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله, and his reward and recompense from Allah is Paradise." [^2] We have repeatedly stated after mentioning the virtues of Surahs that these significant virtues and effects cannot be the result of mere recitation devoid of reflection and action. The aforementioned narrations also testify to this fact, for reciting this Surah without aligning one's life program with it will never expel the spirit of hypocrisy from a person.

[^1]: Majma' al-Bayan, beginning of Surah Al-Munafiqun. [^2]: Thawab al-A'mal, as quoted in Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 5, p. 331.

بِسْمِ اللَّهِ الرَّحْمنِ الرَّحِيمِ إِذا جاءَكَ الْمُنافِقُونَ قالُوا نَشْهَدُ إِنَّكَ لَرَسُولُ اللَّهِ وَ اللَّهُ يَعْلَمُ إِنَّكَ لَرَسُولُهُ وَ اللَّهُ يَشْهَدُ إِنَّ الْمُنافِقِينَ لَكاذِبُونَ (1) اتَّخَذُوا أَيْمانَهُمْ جُنَّةً فَصَدُّوا عَنْ سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ إِنَّهُمْ ساءَ ما كانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ (2) ذلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمْ آمَنُوا ثُمَّ كَفَرُوا فَطُبِعَ عَلى‌ قُلُوبِهِمْ فَهُمْ لا يَفْقَهُونَ (3) وَ إِذا رَأَيْتَهُمْ تُعْجِبُكَ أَجْسامُهُمْ وَ إِنْ يَقُولُوا تَسْمَعْ لِقَوْلِهِمْ كَأَنَّهُمْ خُشُبٌ مُسَنَّدَةٌ يَحْسَبُونَ كُلَّ صَيْحَةٍ عَلَيْهِمْ هُمُ الْعَدُوُّ فَاحْذَرْهُمْ قاتَلَهُمُ اللَّهُ أَنَّى يُؤْفَكُونَ (4)

Commentary

The Source of Hypocrisy and the Signs of the Hypocrites!

Before entering into the commentary of these verses, an introduction seems necessary: the issue of hypocrisy and hypocrites in Islam emerged from the time the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله emigrated to Medina and the foundations of Islam became strong and its victory became apparent. Otherwise, in Mecca, there were almost no hypocrites, because the powerful opponents said and did whatever they wanted openly against Islam; they feared no one and had no need for hypocritical actions.

However, when the influence and expansion of Islam in Medina placed the enemies in a state of weakness and inability, expressing opposition openly became difficult, and sometimes impossible. Therefore, the defeated enemies changed their appearance to continue their destructive programs, outwardly joining the ranks of the Muslims while continuing their deeds in secret.

Fundamentally, the nature of every revolution is such that after a striking victory, it will face ranks of hypocrites; the staunch enemies of yesterday appear as infiltrators today in the garb of apparent friends. From here, one can understand why all these verses related to the hypocrites were revealed in Medina and not in Mecca.

It is also noteworthy that the issue of hypocrisy and hypocrites was not exclusive to the era of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله; rather, every society—especially revolutionary societies—faces it. For this reason, the Quranic analyses and scrutinies of this issue should be studied closely, not as a historical matter, but as a currently needed issue, to draw inspiration for fighting the spirit of hypocrisy and the lines of hypocrites in today's Islamic societies.

Furthermore, one must precisely recognize their signs, which the Quran has described extensively, and through these signs, discover their lines and plots.

Another important point is that the danger of hypocrites to any society is greater than the danger of any enemy. This is because, on one hand, recognizing them is often not easy; on the other hand, they are internal enemies and sometimes penetrate the fabric of society so deeply that separating them is a very difficult task; and thirdly, their various relationships with other members of society make the struggle against them arduous.

For this reason, throughout its history, Islam has received the most blows from hypocrites, and for this reason, the Quran has directed its harshest attacks toward the hypocrites, condemning them more than any other enemy.

With this introduction in mind, we return to the commentary of the verses:

The first thing the Quran mentions here regarding the hypocrites is their false profession of faith, which forms the primary basis of hypocrisy. It says: "When the hypocrites come to you, they say, 'We bear witness that you are indeed the Messenger of Allah'!" (إِذا جاءَكَ الْمُنافِقُونَ قالُوا نَشْهَدُ إِنَّكَ لَرَسُولُ اللَّهِ) [^1] Then the Quran adds: "And Allah knows that you are indeed His Messenger, and Allah bears witness that the hypocrites are surely liars" (وَ اللَّهُ يَعْلَمُ إِنَّكَ لَرَسُولُهُ وَ اللَّهُ يَشْهَدُ إِنَّ الْمُنافِقِينَ لَكاذِبُونَ).

From here, the first sign of hypocrisy becomes clear: the duality of the outward and the inward. They emphatically profess faith with their tongues, but in their hearts, there is absolutely no trace of faith. This lying and falsehood, this duality of the internal and external, forms the main axis of hypocrisy.

It is noteworthy that truthfulness and falsehood are of two types: "informational" truth/falsehood and "informant" truth/falsehood. In the first type, the criterion is whether the statement corresponds to reality. In the second type, the criterion is correspondence with the speaker's belief. This means that if a person gives news that corresponds to reality but is contrary to his own belief, we call it (informant) falsehood; and if it corresponds to his belief, it is truth.

On this basis, the hypocrites' testimony to the messengership of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله was never false in terms of

[^1]: Here "inna" is mentioned with a kasra because the lam of emphasis has entered upon the predicate, and in such a case, it is implicitly preceded (refer to Al-Bayan fi Gharib I'rab al-Quran).

the information itself; it was a reality. However, from the perspective of the speaker and informant, since it was contrary to their belief, it was considered a lie. Therefore, the Quran says: You are the Prophet of Allah, but these people are lying! In other words: the hypocrites did not intend to report the messengership of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, but rather intended to report their own belief in his prophethood, and they were certainly liars in this report.

It is also noteworthy that they used various forms of emphasis in their testimony [^1], and Allah also refutes them with the same tone of decisiveness, indicating that in the face of such decisiveness, such a counter-decisiveness is necessary.

It is also necessary to mention here that "Munafiq" (hypocrite) is originally derived from the root "nafaq" (rhyming with nafkh), meaning penetration and advancement. And "nafaq" (rhyming with shafaq) refers to channels and tunnels dug underground to be used for concealment or escape.

Some commentators have said that many animals, such as the field mouse, fox, and lizard, make two holes for their nests: one obvious, through which they enter and exit, and another hidden, through which they escape if they sense danger. This hidden hole is called "nafiqa" [^2]. Thus, a "Munafiq" is someone who has chosen a mysterious and secret path for himself to penetrate society through secrecy and concealment, and to escape through another way in times of danger.

[^1]: The use of the "nominal sentence" (jumla ismiyya) as well as "inna" and the "lam of emphasis." [^2]: Ruh al-Bayan, Vol. 9, p. 529.

The next verse addresses their second sign, stating: "They have made their oaths a shield, so they bar [others] from the way of Allah" (اتَّخَذُوا أَيْمانَهُمْ جُنَّةً فَصَدُّوا عَنْ سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ). "Indeed, evil is what they used to do" (إِنَّهُمْ ساءَ ما كانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ).

This is because they outwardly express faith and inwardly harbor disbelief, and they create obstacles in the path of guiding people to the true religion. And what action is worse and uglier than this?

"Junnah" (shield) is from the root "jann" (rhyming with fann), which originally means hiding something from the senses. The "Jinn" (rhyming with sinn) is called so because it is an unseen being. Since a "shield" hides a person from the strikes of the enemy's weapon, it is called "junnah" in the Arabic language. Densely wooded gardens are also called "jannat" because their ground is hidden.

In any case, this is one of the effects of hypocrisy: they place themselves under the cover of the holy name of Allah and heavy, solemn oaths to conceal their true faces, attract people's emotions toward themselves, and thereby deceive them and "bar [others] from the way of Allah."

This expression also indicates that they are constantly in a state of war and conflict with the believers, and one should never be deceived by their outward displays and smooth-talking, for the choice of a shield is specific to battlefields.

It is true that in some situations, a person has no choice but to swear an oath, or at least an oath helps to express the importance of the subject at hand, but not a false oath, nor an oath for every little thing—which is the method of the hypocrites.

In Surah At-Tawbah, verse 74, we read: يَحْلِفُونَ بِاللَّهِ ما قالُوا وَ لَقَدْ قالُوا كَلِمَةَ الْكُفْرِ: "They swear by Allah that they did not say [offensive words in the Prophet's absence], while they certainly said the word of disbelief."

Commentators have mentioned two meanings for the phrase "فَصَدُّوا عَنْ سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ": First, turning away from the way of Allah, and second, preventing others from this way. Although combining both meanings in the verse under discussion is possible, given their recourse to false oaths, the second meaning seems more appropriate, as the purpose of these oaths is to deceive others.

In one instance, they establish "Masjid al-Dirar" (the Mosque of Harm), and when asked what their goal was, they swear that they had no intention but good! (Tawbah: 107). In another instance, they express readiness to participate in battles that are nearby and where the prospect of booty is high, but for participating in the field of "Tabuk," which is full of hardships, they bring a thousand excuses and swear that if they were able, they would have marched out with you! (Tawbah: 42).

They not only swear false oaths to the people, but as stated in Surah Al-Mujadila, verse 18, they will even resort to false oaths in the presence of Allah on the Day of Resurrection! This shows that this action has become part of the fabric of their being, such that they do not let go of it even in the arena of the Resurrection and before God!

The next verse addresses the primary cause of such improper actions, adding: "That is because they believed, then disbelieved; therefore their hearts were sealed, so they do not understand" (ذلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمْ آمَنُوا ثُمَّ كَفَرُوا فَطُبِعَ عَلى‌ قُلُوبِهِمْ فَهُمْ لا يَفْقَهُونَ).

A group of commentators believes that the "faith" mentioned here refers to outward faith, while they were disbelievers inwardly. However, the literal meaning of the verse indicates that they initially became true believers, and after tasting the flavor of faith and seeing the signs of the truth of Islam and the Quran, they chose the path of disbelief—but a disbelief coupled with hypocrisy, not open and explicit. This caused Allah to strip them of the sense of discernment, leaving them deprived of understanding realities. For if they had not recognized the truth from the beginning, they would have had an excuse; but after recognizing the truth and believing, if they turn their backs on it, Allah withdraws His success (tawfiq) from them.

In reality, hypocrites are of two groups: one group whose faith was formal and outward from the start, and another group who initially had true faith but then chose the path of apostasy and hypocrisy. The literal meaning of the verse under discussion refers to the second group. In fact, this verse is similar to verse 74 of Surah At-Tawbah, which says: وَ كَفَرُوا بَعْدَ إِسْلامِهِمْ: "And they disbelieved after their [acceptance of] Islam."

In any case, this is the third sign among their signs: they are often deprived of understanding clear realities. It is self-evident that this never leads to predestination (jabr), because they themselves provided the prerequisites for it.

The next verse presents more of their signs, saying: "And when you see them, their bodies [and appearance] impress you" (وَ إِذا رَأَيْتَهُمْ تُعْجِبُكَ أَجْسامُهُمْ). They have a polished appearance and attractive countenances.

Furthermore, they speak so sweetly and attractively that "when they speak, you listen to their speech"! (وَ إِنْ يَقُولُوا تَسْمَعْ لِقَوْلِهِمْ). Where the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله might outwardly be affected by the attractiveness of their words, the situation for others is clear.

This is regarding the outward; as for the inward: "they are as if they were pieces of wood propped up" (كَأَنَّهُمْ خُشُبٌ مُسَنَّدَةٌ). They have soulless bodies, meaningless faces, and hollow frames. They have no independence of their own, no light or purity within, and no firm will, decision, or faith—just like dry wood leaning against a wall! Some commentators have narrated that "Abdullah ibn Ubayy," the leader of the hypocrites, was a large-bodied, handsome, eloquent, and smooth-talking man. When he entered the assembly of the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله with a group of his companions, the companions would be amazed by their appearance and listen to their words. But they (due to the pride and arrogance they had) would go to the side of the wall and lean against it, trying to dominate the assembly with their appearance and speech [^1], and the verse refers to their state.

Then it adds: They are so hollow and lacking in trust in Allah and self-confidence that "they think every cry is against them" (يَحْسَبُونَ كُلَّ صَيْحَةٍ عَلَيْهِمْ). A strange fear and terror always rule over their hearts and souls, and a state of agonizing suspicion and pessimism has permeated their entire spirit. According to the maxim "The traitor is fearful," they are afraid of everything, even their own shadows. This is another sign among the signs of the hypocrites.

And at the end of the verse, it warns the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله: "They are the [real] enemy, so beware of them" (هُمُ الْعَدُوُّ فَاحْذَرْهُمْ). Then it says: "May Allah destroy them! How are they deluded [from the truth]?" (قاتَلَهُمُ اللَّهُ أَنَّى يُؤْفَكُونَ).

It is clear that this expression is not a report, but rather in the form of a curse, mentioned for the purpose of blame, reproach, and humiliation of this group. It is similar to everyday expressions people use regarding one another, as the Quran speaks to them in the language of the people themselves.

Thus, in the verse under discussion, other signs of the hypocrites are presented, including: A deceptive outward appearance coupled with internal emptiness, as well as fear, terror, and suspicion toward every thing and every event.

Ayahs

وَإِذَا قِيلَ لَهُمْ تَعَالَوْا۟ يَسْتَغْفِرْ لَكُمْ رَسُولُ ٱللَّهِ لَوَّوْا۟ رُءُوسَهُمْ وَرَأَيْتَهُمْ يَصُدُّونَ وَهُم مُّسْتَكْبِرُونَ (٥)
When they are told, ‘Come, that Allah’s Apostle may plead for forgiveness for you,’ they twist their heads, and you see them turn away disdainfully. (5)
سَوَآءٌ عَلَيْهِمْ أَسْتَغْفَرْتَ لَهُمْ أَمْ لَمْ تَسْتَغْفِرْ لَهُمْ لَن يَغْفِرَ ٱللَّهُ لَهُمْ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يَهْدِى ٱلْقَوْمَ ٱلْفَـٰسِقِينَ (٦)
It is the same for them whether you plead for forgiveness for them, or do not plead for forgiveness for them: Allah will never forgive them. Indeed Allah does not guide the transgressing lot. (6)
هُمُ ٱلَّذِينَ يَقُولُونَ لَا تُنفِقُوا۟ عَلَىٰ مَنْ عِندَ رَسُولِ ٱللَّهِ حَتَّىٰ يَنفَضُّوا۟ ۗ وَلِلَّهِ خَزَآئِنُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ وَلَـٰكِنَّ ٱلْمُنَـٰفِقِينَ لَا يَفْقَهُونَ (٧)
They are the ones who say, ‘Do not spend on those who are with the Apostle of Allah until they scatter off [from around him].’ Yet to Allah belong the treasuries of the heavens and the earth, but the hypocrites do not understand. (7)
يَقُولُونَ لَئِن رَّجَعْنَآ إِلَى ٱلْمَدِينَةِ لَيُخْرِجَنَّ ٱلْأَعَزُّ مِنْهَا ٱلْأَذَلَّ ۚ وَلِلَّهِ ٱلْعِزَّةُ وَلِرَسُولِهِۦ وَلِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَلَـٰكِنَّ ٱلْمُنَـٰفِقِينَ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ (٨)
They say, ‘When we return to the city, the mighty will surely expel the weak from it.’ Yet all might belongs to Allah and His Apostle and the faithful, but the hypocrites do not know. (8)

Circumstances of Revelation

A detailed account regarding the circumstances of the revelation of the above verses has appeared in books of history, Hadith, and Tafsir, the summary of which is as follows: After the Battle of Banu al-Mustaliq (a war that took place in the year 6 AH in the land of "Qadid").

Two Muslims, one from the Ansar and the other from the Muhajirun, had a dispute while drawing water from a well. One called upon the tribe of Ansar for help, and the other called upon the Muhajirun. A man from the Muhajirun came to his friend's aid, and "Abdullah ibn Ubayy," who was among the notorious leaders of the hypocrites, rushed to the aid of the Ansari man. A heated verbal altercation broke out between the two. Abdullah ibn Ubayy became severely enraged and, while a group of his people were with him, said: "We gave refuge to this group of Muhajirun and helped them, but our situation is like the famous proverb that says: 'Fatten your dog and it will eat you!' By Allah, لَئِنْ رَجَعْنا إِلَى الْمَدِينَةِ لَيُخْرِجَنَّ الْأَعَزُّ مِنْهَا الْأَذَلَ‌: 'If we return to Medina, the more honorable will surely expel therefrom the meaner,'" and by "the more honorable" he meant himself and his followers, and by "the meaner" he meant the Muhajirun. Then he turned to those around him

and said: "This is the result of what you have done to yourselves; you gave this group a place in your city and divided your wealth with them. Had you not given the leftovers of your food to the likes of this man (referring to the Muhajir man involved in the dispute), they would not have ridden upon your necks; they would have left your land and joined their own tribes!" At this point, "Zayd ibn Arqam," who was a young youth at the time, turned to Abdullah ibn Ubayy and said: "By Allah, you are the mean and the few! And Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله is in Divine honor and the love of the Muslims, and by Allah, I shall not love you after this." Abdullah shouted: "Be quiet! You should be playing, O child!" Zayd ibn Arqam went to the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله and related the incident.

The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله sent someone for Abdullah and asked: "What is this that they have related to me?" Abdullah said: "By the God who revealed the Heavenly Book to you, I said nothing! And Zayd is lying."

A group of the Ansar who were present said: "O Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله, Abdullah is our elder; do not accept the word of a child among the children of the Ansar against him." The Prophet accepted their excuse. At this point, the tribe of Ansar blamed Zayd ibn Arqam.

The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله ordered the march to begin. One of the elders of the Ansar named "Usayd" came to him and said: "O Messenger of Allah! You have started the march at an unusual hour." He replied: "Yes, have you not heard what your companion Abdullah said? He said that when he returns to Medina, the more honorable will expel the meaner."

Usayd said: "You, O Messenger of Allah, if you wish, shall expel him. By Allah, You are the honorable and he is the mean." Then he said: "O Messenger of Allah, be lenient with him."

The words of Abdullah ibn Ubayy reached his son. He came to the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله and said: "I have heard you intend to kill my father. If it is so, command me myself to sever his head and bring it to you! For the people know that no one is more dutiful to his parents than I. I fear that someone else might kill him and I would not be able to look at my father's killer afterward, and God forbid, I might kill him and thus have killed a believer and go to Hell!"

The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said: "The matter of killing your father is not on the table; as long as he is with us, treat him with leniency and kindness."

Then the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله ordered the army to continue the march all that day and all night. The next day when the sun rose, He ordered a halt. The soldiers were so exhausted that as soon as they put their heads to the ground, they fell into a deep sleep (and the Prophet's goal was for the people to forget yesterday's incident and the words of Abdullah ibn Ubayy...).

Finally, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله entered Medina. Zayd ibn Arqam says: "I stayed at home out of intense grief and shame and did not come out." At this time, Surah Al-Munafiqun was revealed, confirming Zayd and belied Abdullah. The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله took Zayd's ear and said: "O youth! Allah has confirmed your word, as well as what your ears heard and your heart preserved. Allah has revealed verses of the Quran regarding what you said."

At this time, Abdullah ibn Ubayy had reached the outskirts of Medina. When he wanted to enter the city, his son came and blocked his father's path. He said: "Woe to you, what are you doing?" His son said: "By Allah, you shall not enter Medina except by the permission of the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله, and today you shall know who is the honorable and who is the mean!!"

Abdullah sent a complaint about his son to the Messenger of Allah. The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله sent word to the son to let his father enter the city. The son said: "Now that the permission of the Messenger of Allah has come, there is no obstacle."

Abdullah entered the city, but not more than a few days passed before he fell ill and passed away (and perhaps he died of grief). When these verses were revealed and Abdullah's lie became apparent, some said to him: "Severe verses have been revealed regarding you; go to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله so he may seek forgiveness for you." Abdullah shook his head and said: "You told me: 'Believe,' and I believed. You said:

'Give Zakat,' and I gave. Nothing remains but for you to say: 'Prostrate to Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله!'" And here the verse وَ إِذا قِيلَ لَهُمْ تَعالَوْا was revealed [1].

Commentary

Other Signs of the Hypocrites

These verses continue the description of the actions of the hypocrites and their various signs. It says: "And when it is said to them, 'Come, the Messenger of Allah will ask forgiveness for you,' they turn their heads away in mockery, pride, and arrogance, and you see them turning away while they are arrogant" (وَ إِذا قِيلَ لَهُمْ تَعالَوْا يَسْتَغْفِرْ لَكُمْ رَسُولُ اللَّهِ لَوَّوْا رُؤُسَهُمْ وَ رَأَيْتَهُمْ يَصُدُّونَ وَ هُمْ مُسْتَكْبِرُونَ‌).

Indeed, in the face of the slips they commit, while they have the opportunity to repent and make amends,

[1]: "Majma' al-Bayan" under the discussed verses; "Al-Kamil fi al-Tarikh" by Ibn al-Athir, Vol. 2, p. 192; "Sirat Ibn Hisham," Vol. 3, p. 302 (with slight variations).

pride and arrogance do not allow them to seek rectification. A clear example of this was the same Abdullah ibn Ubayy whose strange story we read in the circumstances of revelation. When he spoke those very ugly and improper words regarding the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the Muhajir believers—that when we return to Medina, the more honorable will expel the meaner—and the verses of the Quran were revealed severely rebuking him, they suggested to him to come to the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله so he might seek forgiveness for him from Allah's threshold. He spoke another improper word, the gist of which was: "You said: 'Believe,' and I believed; you said: 'Give Zakat,' and I gave; nothing is left but for you to say: 'Prostrate to Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله!'" It is clear that the spirit of Islam is submission to the Truth, and pride and arrogance always prevent this submission. For this reason, one of the signs of the hypocrites—rather, one of the motives for hypocrisy—can be considered this selfishness, self-superiority, and pride.

"Lawwaw" (لَوَّوْا) is from the root "layy" (لى), which originally means "twisting a rope," and accordingly, it is also used to mean turning the head or shaking the head.

"Yasuddun" (يَصُدُّونَ), as we have said before, is used in two senses: "preventing" and "turning away." In the context of the verse under discussion, the second meaning is appropriate, while for the previous verse, the first meaning was suitable.

In the next verse, to remove any ambiguity in this regard, it adds: "Supposing they were to come to you and you were to seek forgiveness for them, the ground for forgiveness does not exist within them. Therefore, 'It is all the same for them whether you ask forgiveness for them or do not ask forgiveness for them; Allah will never forgive them!'" (سَواءٌ عَلَيْهِمْ أَسْتَغْفَرْتَ لَهُمْ أَمْ لَمْ تَسْتَغْفِرْ لَهُمْ لَنْ يَغْفِرَ اللَّهُ لَهُمْ‌).

The reason for this is that "Indeed, Allah does not guide the defiantly disobedient people" (إِنَّ اللَّهَ لا يَهْدِي الْقَوْمَ الْفاسِقِينَ‌).

In other words, the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله seeking of forgiveness is not the "sufficient cause" for forgiveness, but rather a "requisite." It only takes effect if the conducive ground and necessary capacity are provided. If they truly repent, change their course, dismount from the steed of pride and arrogance, and bow their heads in submission to the Truth, the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله seeking of forgiveness and his intercession will certainly be effective; otherwise, it will not have the slightest effect.

A similar meaning appears in verse 80 of Surah At-Tawbah, which says regarding another group of hypocrites: اسْتَغْفِرْ لَهُمْ أَوْ لا تَسْتَغْفِرْ لَهُمْ إِنْ تَسْتَغْفِرْ لَهُمْ سَبْعِينَ مَرَّةً فَلَنْ يَغْفِرَ اللَّهُ لَهُمْ ذلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمْ كَفَرُوا بِاللَّهِ وَ رَسُولِهِ وَ اللَّهُ لا يَهْدِي الْقَوْمَ الْفاسِقِينَ‌.

"Whether you ask forgiveness for them or do not ask forgiveness for them [it is the same]; even if you ask forgiveness for them seventy times, Allah will never forgive them. That is because they disbelieved in Allah and His Messenger, and Allah does not guide the defiantly disobedient people."

It is clear that the number seventy is a number of multiplication, meaning no matter how much you seek forgiveness for them, it will be of no use.

It is also evident that by "Fasiq" (defiantly disobedient), it does not mean every kind of sinner—for the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله came to save sinners—but rather it refers to that group of sinners who persist in sin, are stubborn, and are arrogant against the Truth.

Then it points to one of their very ugly statements, which is considered the clearest evidence of their hypocrisy, saying: "They are the ones who say,

'Do not spend on those who are with the Messenger of Allah until they disperse'" (هُمُ الَّذِينَ يَقُولُونَ لا تُنْفِقُوا عَلى‌ مَنْ عِنْدَ رَسُولِ اللَّهِ حَتَّى يَنْفَضُّوا).

"Unaware that to Allah belong the treasuries of the heavens and the earth, but the hypocrites do not understand" (وَ لِلَّهِ خَزائِنُ السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضِ وَ لكِنَّ الْمُنافِقِينَ لا يَفْقَهُونَ‌).

These wretched ones do not know that whatever anyone has is from Allah, and all servants eat from His spread table. If the "Ansar" can give refuge to the "Muhajirun" and share their wealth with them, this is the greatest honor bestowed upon them. Not only should they not place a burden of favor, but they should thank Allah for this great success. However, as we read in the circumstances of revelation, the hypocrites of Medina had a different logic.

Then it points to another of their most repulsive statements, adding: "They say, 'If we return to Medina, the more honorable will surely expel therefrom the meaner!'" (يَقُولُونَ لَئِنْ رَجَعْنا إِلَى الْمَدِينَةِ لَيُخْرِجَنَّ الْأَعَزُّ مِنْهَا الْأَذَلَ‌).

This is the very speech that came out of the polluted mouth of "Abdullah ibn Ubayy," and his intention was that we, the residents of Medina, will expel the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله and the Muhajir believers. The "return to Medina" referred to the return from the Battle of Banu al-Mustaliq, which was mentioned in detail in the circumstances of revelation.

It is true that this statement was issued by one person, but since all hypocrites followed this same line and policy, the Quran expresses it in the plural form and says: يقولون ... (They say...).

Then the Quran gives them a crushing response, saying: "And to Allah belongs [all] honor,

and to His Messenger, and to the believers, but the hypocrites do not know" (وَ لِلَّهِ الْعِزَّةُ وَ لِرَسُولِهِ وَ لِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَ لكِنَّ الْمُنافِقِينَ لا يَعْلَمُونَ‌).

It was not only the hypocrites of Medina who said this to the Muhajir believers; before them, the leaders of Quraysh in Mecca used to say: "If we place this small group of poor Muslims under an economic blockade, or expel them from Mecca, the matter is finished!" Today, too, colonialist governments, under the delusion that they possess the treasuries of the heavens and the earth, say that nations that do not surrender to us must be placed under economic blockade until they come to their senses and surrender! These blind-hearted ones of history, whose methods were the same yesterday and today, are unaware that with one command from Allah, all their wealth and resources will be blown away and their hollow honor will fall into ruin.

In any case, this way of thinking (considering oneself honorable and others mean, and considering oneself the benefactor and others needy) is a hypocritical thought arising from pride and arrogance on one hand, and the assumption of independence from Allah on the other. If they were acquainted with the reality of servitude and considered Allah's ownership over everything as certain, they would never have fallen into these dangerous mistakes.

It is noteworthy that in the previous verse regarding the hypocrites, the expression "لا يَفْقَهُونَ‌" (they do not understand) is used, and here "لا يَعْلَمُونَ‌" (they do not know). This difference in expression might be to avoid repetition, which is contrary to eloquence, and it might also be because understanding the issue of Allah's ownership of all the treasuries of the heavens and the earth is a more complex matter requiring more precision and understanding,

whereas the fact that honor belongs exclusively to Allah, the Prophet, and the believers is not hidden from anyone.

Key Points

1- Ten Signs of the Hypocrite!

From the sum of the above verses, several signs for the hypocrites can be derived, which can be summarized into ten signs:

1- Explicit and blatant lying (وَ اللَّهُ يَشْهَدُ إِنَّ الْمُنافِقِينَ لَكاذِبُونَ‌).

2- Using false oaths to lead people astray (اتَّخَذُوا أَيْمانَهُمْ جُنَّةً).

3- Lack of understanding of realities due to abandoning the religion of Truth after recognizing it (لا يَفْقَهُونَ‌).

4- Having an adorned appearance and a smooth tongue, despite being empty within (وَ إِذا رَأَيْتَهُمْ تُعْجِبُكَ أَجْسامُهُمْ‌).

5- Uselessness in society and lack of flexibility toward the Truth, like a piece of dry wood (كَأَنَّهُمْ خُشُبٌ مُسَنَّدَةٌ).

6- Suspicion, fear, and terror of every incident and everything because of being treacherous (يَحْسَبُونَ كُلَّ صَيْحَةٍ عَلَيْهِمْ‌).

7- Mocking and ridiculing the Truth (لَوَّوْا رُؤُسَهُمْ‌).

8- Defiant disobedience and sin (إِنَّ اللَّهَ لا يَهْدِي الْقَوْمَ الْفاسِقِينَ‌).

9- Considering oneself the owner of everything and imagining others to be in need of oneself (هُمُ الَّذِينَ يَقُولُونَ لا تُنْفِقُوا عَلى‌ مَنْ عِنْدَ رَسُولِ اللَّهِ حَتَّى يَنْفَضُّوا).

10- Imagining oneself as honorable and others as mean (لَيُخْرِجَنَّ الْأَعَزُّ

مِنْهَا الْأَذَلَ‌).

Undoubtedly, the signs of a hypocrite are not limited to these; from other verses of the Quran, Islamic narrations, and Nahj al-Balagha, many other signs can be derived. Even in daily interactions, one can discover other traits and characteristics of them. However, what has appeared in the verses of this Surah is an important and significant portion of these traits.

In Nahj al-Balagha, there is a sermon specifically describing the hypocrites. In part of that sermon, it says:

"O servants of Allah, I advise you to have Taqwa (piety) and I warn you against the hypocrites, for they are the strayed and the straying, the erring and the misleading.

Every day they appear in a new color, and they show themselves in different appearances and tongues.

They use every means to deceive and break you, and they lie in wait for you at every ambush.

They are evil within and fair without, and they constantly step secretly to deceive people, moving through bypasses.

Their speech is seemingly healing, but their actions are an incurable pain.

They envy the prosperity and comfort of people, and if someone is afflicted with a calamity, they are happy.

They always make the hopeful lose hope and recite the verse of despair everywhere.

They have a victim on every path! And a way to penetrate every heart! And they shed artificial tears for every tragedy!

They lend praise and eulogy to one another and expect a reward from each other.

They are persistent in their requests and shameless in their blame, and whenever they judge, they exceed the limits.

Against every truth, they have fashioned a falsehood; against every proof, a doubt; for every living thing, a cause of death; for every door, a key; and for every night, a lamp! To reach their greed and warm their market and sell their goods at the highest price, they sow the seeds of despair in hearts.

They make their falsehood appear like the truth and adopt the path of deception in their descriptions.

They make the way to reach their desire appear easy, and the way to exit their trap narrow and winding. They are the party of Satan and the sparks of the fire of Hell! Just as Allah has said: أُولئِكَ حِزْبُ الشَّيْطانِ أَلا إِنَّ حِزْبَ الشَّيْطانِ هُمُ الْخاسِرُونَ 'They are the party of Satan. Unquestionably, the party of Satan—they are the losers'" [1]! In this eloquent sermon, many of their traits are mentioned which complete the previous discussions.

2- The Danger of the Hypocrites

As we said in the introduction to this discussion, the hypocrites are the most dangerous individuals

[1]: "Nahj al-Balagha," Sermon 194 (the full text of the sermon was omitted for brevity).

in any society because, firstly, they live within societies and are aware of all secrets.

Secondly, identifying them is not always easy, and sometimes they present themselves in the clothing of a friend such that one cannot believe otherwise.

Thirdly, because their true face is unknown to many people, direct confrontation and explicit struggle against them is difficult.

Fourthly, they have various ties with the believers (ties of marriage, kinship, and others), and the existence of these ties makes the struggle against them more complex. Fifthly, they stab from behind and their blows are unexpected.

These aspects and others cause them to bring irreparable damage to societies, and for this reason, there must be precise and extensive planning to repel their evil.

In a Hadith, it is narrated that the Prophet said:

"I do not fear for my Ummah from a believer nor a polytheist. As for the believer, Allah prevents him by his faith, and as for the polytheist, Allah disgraces him by his polytheism. But I fear for you every hypocrite who is learned of tongue; he says what you recognize and does what you find repulsive." [1] Regarding the hypocrites, we have had detailed discussions in Volume 1 (under verses 8 to 16

[1]: "Safinat al-Bihar," Vol. 2, p. 606, root "nafaqa"—a similar meaning also appears in "Nahj al-Balagha," Letter 27.

of Al-Baqarah), in Volume 8 under verses 60 to 85 of Surah At-Tawbah (pages 19 to 72), in Volume 17 under verses 12 to 17 of Surah Al-Ahzab (pages 224 to 232), and in Volume 7 under verses 43 to 45 of Surah At-Tawbah (pages 428 to 456).

In short, there is hardly any group about whom the Quran has discussed so much, recounting their signs, actions, and dangers. This extensive investment by the Quran in this regard is proof of the extraordinary danger of the hypocrites.

3- The Hypocrite is Rigid and Brittle

Throughout life, storms blow and roaring waves appear.

Believers, using the power of faith and trust, and correct plans—sometimes through tactical retreat and sometimes through successive attacks—pass through them and emerge victorious. But the stubborn and obstinate hypocrite stands until he breaks. In a Hadith (from the Noble Prophet of Islam), it is said:

"The example of the believer is like that of a plant; the wind does not cease to bend it, and the believer does not cease to be afflicted by trials. And the example of the hypocrite is like that of the cedar tree; it does not shake until it is uprooted." [1]!

4- Honor is Exclusive to Allah and His Friends

Although in everyday Persian "Izzat" (عزت) means respect and reputation or being

[1]: "Sahih Muslim," Vol. 4, p. 2163 (Chapter: The Example of the Believer is like the Plant); a similar theme with slight variation also appears in Tafsir "Ruh al-Bayan," Vol. 9, p. 533.

precious, in the Arabic language it is not so. Rather, "Izzat" means invincible power. It is noteworthy that in the above verses and in verse 10 of Surah Fatir, "Izzat" is considered exclusively for Allah, and in the verses under discussion, it adds: "and for His Messenger and the believers," because the saints and friends of Allah also have a reflection of His honor and are reliant upon Him.

For this reason, in Islamic narrations, emphasis is placed on the fact that a believer should not provide the means for his own humiliation; Allah has willed for him to be honorable, and he too must strive to preserve this honor.

In a Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام in the commentary of this very verse (وَ لِلَّهِ الْعِزَّةُ وَ لِرَسُولِهِ وَ لِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ‌), we read:

"The believer is honorable and shall not be humiliated... The believer is firmer than a mountain; for the mountain can be carved with pickaxes, but nothing can be carved away from the believer's religion." [1] In another Hadith from the same Imam, we read:

"It is not fitting for a believer to humiliate himself." It was asked, "How does he humiliate himself?" He replied, "By taking on what he cannot bear!" [2] And again in a third Hadith from him:

"Indeed, Allah, Blessed and Exalted be He, has delegated all his affairs to the believer, but He has not delegated to him to humiliate himself. Have you not seen the word of Allah, Glorified and Exalted be He, here: 'And to Allah belongs honor, and to His Messenger, and to the believers'? Thus, it is fitting for the believer to be honorable and not to be humiliated." [1] In this regard, we had another discussion under verse 10 of Surah Fatir (Vol. 18, p. 197).

[1] and [2]: "Al-Kafi," as quoted in "Nur al-Thaqalayn," Vol. 5, p. 336. [1]: "Al-Kafi," as quoted in "Nur al-Thaqalayn," Vol. 5, p. 336.

Ayahs

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ لَا تُلْهِكُمْ أَمْوَٰلُكُمْ وَلَآ أَوْلَـٰدُكُمْ عَن ذِكْرِ ٱللَّهِ ۚ وَمَن يَفْعَلْ ذَٰلِكَ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْخَـٰسِرُونَ (٩)
O you who have faith! Do not let your possessions and children distract you from the remembrance of Allah, and whoever does that—it is they who are the losers. (9)
وَأَنفِقُوا۟ مِن مَّا رَزَقْنَـٰكُم مِّن قَبْلِ أَن يَأْتِىَ أَحَدَكُمُ ٱلْمَوْتُ فَيَقُولَ رَبِّ لَوْلَآ أَخَّرْتَنِىٓ إِلَىٰٓ أَجَلٍ قَرِيبٍ فَأَصَّدَّقَ وَأَكُن مِّنَ ٱلصَّـٰلِحِينَ (١٠)
Spend out of what We have provided you before death comes to any of you, whereat he might say, ‘My Lord, why did You not respite me for a short time so that I could give charity and become one of the righteous!’ (10)
وَلَن يُؤَخِّرَ ٱللَّهُ نَفْسًا إِذَا جَآءَ أَجَلُهَا ۚ وَٱللَّهُ خَبِيرٌۢ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ (١١)
But Allah will never respite anyone when his time has come, and Allah is well aware of what you do. (11)

Commentary

Property and Children Must Not Divert You from the Remembrance of Allah!

Since one of the significant factors of hypocrisy is the love of the world and an excessive attachment to wealth and children, these verses—which are the final verses of Surah "Al-Munafiqun"—warn the believers against such immoderate affection, stating: "O you who have believed, let not your properties and your children divert you from the remembrance of Allah" (يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لَا تُلْهِكُمْ أَمْوَالُكُمْ وَلَا أَوْلَادُكُمْ عَنْ ذِكْرِ اللَّهِ).

"And whoever does that—then those are the losers" (وَمَنْ يَفْعَلْ ذَلِكَ فَأُولَئِكَ هُمُ الْخَاسِرُونَ).

It is true that wealth and children are Divine blessings, but only as long as they are used as means in the path of Allah and for attaining salvation. However, if excessive attachment to them creates a barrier between man and God, they are considered the greatest calamity. As we observed in the account of the hypocrites in the preceding verses, one of the factors of their deviation was this very love for the world.

In a hadith from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام, this concept is depicted in the clearest manner, where he says:

مَا ذِئْبَانِ ضَارِيَانِ فِي غَنَمٍ لَيْسَ لَهَا رَاعٍ، هَذَا فِي أَوَّلِهَا وَ هَذَا فِي آخِرِهَا بِأَسْرَعَ فِيهَا مِنْ حُبِّ الْمَالِ وَ الشَّرَفِ فِي دِينِ الْمُؤْمِنِ:

"Two ravenous wolves in a shepherdless flock of sheep—one at the front of the flock and the other at the rear—do not cause as much damage as the love of wealth and status causes to the religion of a believer" [1]! Regarding what is meant by "the remembrance of Allah" here, commentators have mentioned many possibilities.

[1]: Usul al-Kafi, Vol. 2, Chapter: "Love of the World," Hadith 3.

Some have interpreted it as the five daily prayers, some as gratitude for blessings, patience in trials, and contentment with Divine decree, and others as Hajj, Zakat, and the recitation of the Quran. Some have interpreted it as all obligatory duties. However, it is clear that "remembrance of Allah" has a broad meaning that encompasses all of these and more; therefore, the aforementioned interpretations are examples of its prominent manifestations.

The expression "losers" (الْخَاسِرُونَ) is used because the love of the world preoccupies man to such an extent that he spends his existential capital on fleeting pleasures—and sometimes on illusions and fantasies—and leaves this world empty-handed, while despite possessing great capital, he did nothing for his eternal life.

Following this stern warning to the believers, He issues the command to spend in the way of Allah, saying: "And spend [in the way of Allah] from what We have provided you before death comes to one of you and he says, 'My Lord, if only You would delay me for a brief term so I would give charity and be among the righteous'" (وَأَنْفِقُوا مِنْ مَا رَزَقْنَاكُمْ مِنْ قَبْلِ أَنْ يَأْتِيَ أَحَدَكُمُ الْمَوْتُ فَيَقُولَ رَبِّ لَوْلَا أَخَّرْتَنِي إِلَى أَجَلٍ قَرِيبٍ فَأَصَّدَّقَ وَأَكُنْ مِنَ الصَّالِحِينَ) [1]. Although some have interpreted the command to spend here as the obligation to hasten the payment of Zakat, it is clear that the verse's intent includes every kind of obligatory and recommended spending that serves as a means of salvation for man in the Hereafter.

[1]: It is noteworthy that in the above verse, "أَصَّدَّقَ" (assaddaqa) is in the subjunctive mood (mansub) and "أَكُنْ" (akun) is in the jussive mood (majzum), even though they are conjoined. This is because "أَكُنْ" is conjoined to the underlying grammatical position of "أَصَّدَّقَ", as if it were: "If You delay me, I will give charity and be among the righteous."

Interestingly, at the end of the verse, it says: "so I would give charity and be among the righteous." This expression demonstrates the profound impact of spending on a person's righteousness. Although some have interpreted "being righteous" here as performing the "Hajj" rituals—and this is explicitly mentioned in some narrations—these too are examples of prominent manifestations.

The phrase "before death comes to one of you" (مِنْ قَبْلِ أَنْ يَأْتِيَ أَحَدَكُمُ الْمَوْتُ) refers to man being on the threshold of death and the appearance of its signs, for man does not say these words after death, but rather at the moment of death's approach.

The expression "from what We have provided you" (مِنْ مَا رَزَقْنَاكُمْ), besides not being limited to wealth but encompassing all blessings, conveys the reality that all of these are from another source and "this trust is with us for a few days." Therefore, what is the meaning of being stingy?

In any case, there are many who, when they gain purgatorial (barzakh) vision and see themselves in the final moments of life and on the threshold of the Resurrection—when the veils of negligence and ignorance are removed from their eyes and they see they must leave their wealth and capital behind without being able to take any provision from it for this long journey—become regretful. The fire of remorse falls upon their souls, and they request a return to life, even if it be a brief and fleeting return, to make amends. But they are met with rejection, for it is the Divine Sunnah (law) that there is no return on this path!

Therefore, in the final verse, He states with absolute decisiveness: "But Allah will never delay a soul when its term has come" (وَلَنْ يُؤَخِّرَ اللَّهُ نَفْسًا إِذَا جَاءَ أَجَلُهَا).

There is no moving it forward or backward by even a single hour, as mentioned in other verses of the Quran. In Surah Al-A'raf, verse 34, we read: "When their term has come, they will not remain behind an hour, nor will they precede [it]" (فَإِذَا جَاءَ أَجَلُهُمْ لَا يَسْتَأْخِرُونَ سَاعَةً وَلَا يَسْتَقْدِمُونَ).

Finally, He concludes the verse with this sentence: "And Allah is Acquainted with what you do" (وَاللَّهُ خَبِيرٌ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ).

He has recorded all of them for reward and punishment and will recompense you for all of them.

Key Points

1- The Way to Overcome Anxieties

In the accounts of the great scholar, "Sheikh Abdullah Shushtari," a contemporary of the late "Allamah Majlisi," it is written that he had a son whom he loved dearly. This son became severely ill. His father, the late Sheikh Abdullah, was distressed when he came to the mosque to perform the Friday prayer. When, according to Islamic instruction, he recited Surah Al-Munafiqun in the second rak'ah and reached this verse: "O you who have believed, let not your properties and your children divert you from the remembrance of Allah," he repeated the verse several times (repeating Quranic verses in prayer is permissible). When he finished the prayer, some of his companions asked about the reason for this repetition. He said: "When I reached this verse, I thought of my son, and by repeating it, I rose to struggle against my self (nafs). I struggled so much that I imagined my son had died and his body was before me, yet I was not heedless of God. It was then that I stopped repeating the verse" [1]!

2- "Ideological" and "Practical" Hypocrisy

Hypocrisy (nifaq) has a broad meaning that encompasses any duality between the internal and external. Its most prominent manifestation is ideological hypocrisy, which the verses about hypocrites usually address; this pertains to those who outwardly profess faith but inwardly conceal polytheism and disbelief.

However, practical hypocrisy pertains to those whose internal belief is Islam, but they perform actions contrary to this internal commitment, showing a duality between their inner and outer faces, such as: breaking promises, lying, and betraying trusts. Thus, in a hadith from the Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, it is stated:

ثَلَاثٌ مَنْ كُنَّ فِيهِ كَانَ مُنَافِقاً، وَ إِنْ صَامَ وَ صَلَّى، وَ زَعَمَ أَنَّهُ مُسْلِمٌ: مَنْ إِذَا اؤْتُمِنَ خَانَ، وَ إِذَا حَدَّثَ كَذَبَ، وَ إِذَا وَعَدَ، أَخْلَفَ

"There are three traits which, if found in anyone, make him a hypocrite, even if he fasts, prays, and considers himself a Muslim: he who betrays when trusted, lies when he speaks, and breaks his promise when he makes one" [2]. In another hadith from the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله, we read:

مَا زَادَ خُشُوعُ الْجَسَدِ عَلَى مَا فِي الْقَلْبِ فَهُوَ عِنْدَنَا نِفَاقٌ

"Whatever the outward humility of the body exceeds what is in the heart, that is hypocrisy in our view" [1]. And elsewhere from Imam Ali ibn al-Husayn عليه السلام, we read:

إِنَّ الْمُنَافِقَ يَنْهَى وَ لَا يَنْتَهِي وَ يَأْمُرُ بِمَا لَا يَأْتِي

"The hypocrite forbids [evil] but does not desist [from it himself], and commands [good] what he does not perform [himself]" [2]! And among the significant branches of practical hypocrisy is the issue of polytheism (shirk) and ostentation (riya), which is also mentioned in Islamic narrations.

O Allah! The scope of hypocrisy is vast and wide, and there is no way of salvation from it except by Your grace and mercy. Help us on this winding path! O Lord! Make us among those who do not burn in the fire of regret at the time of bidding farewell to the world and do not request to return. O God! To You belong the treasuries of the heavens and the earth, and honor belongs to You and Your friends. Make us honorable by the blessing of faith, and grant us a portion from Your endless treasuries—Amen, O Lord of the Worlds.

(End of Surah Al-Munafiqun) 18 Ramadan 1406

[1]: Usul al-Kafi, Vol. 2 (Chapter: Description of Hypocrisy, Hadith 6). [2]: Same source, Hadith 3.
